tweets.title: India Targets 5 Indigenous Small Modular Reactors by 2033 The Government has announced plans to make at least five indigenous Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) operational by 2033, as part of its long-term nuclear energy strategy. BARC is developing a 220 MW Bharat SMR, a 55 MW SMR, and a high-temperature gas-cooled reactor capable of producing hydrogen. India aims to expand its nuclear power capacity from 8.78 GW today to ~22 GW by 2031–32, with a long-term target of 100 GW by 2047 under the Nuclear Energy Mission.
tweets.content:
India Targets 5 Indigenous Small Modular Reactors by 2033

The Government has announced plans to make at least five indigenous Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) operational by 2033, as part of its long-term nuclear energy strategy. BARC is developing a 220 MW Bharat SMR, a 55 MW SMR, and a high-temperature gas-cooled reactor capable of producing hydrogen.

India aims to expand its nuclear power capacity from 8.78 GW today to ~22 GW by 2031–32, with a long-term target of 100 GW by 2047 under the Nuclear Energy Mission.

tweets.title: India's Bharat Forge has signed an MoU with FLYING WHALES of France-Canada to jointly develop and manufacture heavy-lift airships in India for defence and strategic applications. The partnership will focus on the LCA60T platform—a next-generation airship capable of carrying up to 60 tonnes, featuring vertical take-off and landing (VTOL), hybrid-electric propulsion, and operations with minimal ground infrastructure. The airships are expected to support: 🔹 Logistics to forward military bases 🔹 Transport of heavy equipment and supplies 🔹 ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance & Reconnaissance) missions 🔹 Communication relay 🔹 Humanitarian assistance & disaster relief 🔹 Rapid deployment in remote border regions
tweets.content:
India's Bharat Forge has signed an MoU with FLYING WHALES of France-Canada to jointly develop and manufacture heavy-lift airships in India for defence and strategic applications.

The partnership will focus on the LCA60T platform—a next-generation airship capable of carrying up to 60 tonnes, featuring vertical take-off and landing (VTOL), hybrid-electric propulsion, and operations with minimal ground infrastructure.

The airships are expected to support:
🔹 Logistics to forward military bases
🔹 Transport of heavy equipment and supplies
🔹 ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance & Reconnaissance) missions
🔹 Communication relay
🔹 Humanitarian assistance & disaster relief
🔹 Rapid deployment in remote border regions

tweets.title: There must be zero tolerance for terrorism. Nor should there be any doubt that acts of terrorism will have consequences, EAM Jaishankar at Asean Region Forum meet in Manila
tweets.content:
There must be zero tolerance for terrorism. Nor should there be any doubt that acts of terrorism will have consequences, EAM Jaishankar at Asean Region Forum meet in Manila

Dr. S. Jaishankar (@DrSJaishankar)

Spoke at the 33rd ASEAN Regional Forum (ARF) Ministerial Meeting today in Manila.

➡️ Recognised the importance of ARF for discussing political & security issues in the Indo-Pacific, especially in these complex times.

➡️ Welcomed the Manila Plan of Action and highlighted that its five pillars are aligned with India’s own approach to the Indo-Pacific.

➡️ India promotes a collaborative approach to disaster relief, sharing best practices, developing common SOPs and undertaking real-time & table top exercises.

➡️ Shared that India advocates for the Coalition for Disaster Resilient Infrastructure, regularly deploys field hospitals, provides search & rescue, sends relief materials & medical supplies.

➡️ India has undertaken rehabilitation recently ranging from the cyclone in Sri Lanka, the Philippines and Jamaica to earthquakes in Myanmar, Venezuela and Afghanistan.

➡️ There must be zero tolerance for terrorism. Nor should there be any doubt that acts of terrorism will have consequences.

➡️ We should prioritize choking the financial resources that fuel it. India co-chairs the EWG with Malaysia in ADMM+, taking the lead in producing a compendium of best practices.

➡️ On maritime security, shared that the information Fusion Centre in India, the Indo-Pacific Maritime Domain Awareness initiative, deployment of Indian forces from the North Arabian Sea to the Western Pacific, are making a valuable contribution to counter piracy, trafficking and narcotics trade as well as secure maritime commerce.

➡️ Stressed the need for the International community to be on guard against those who undermine non-proliferation and disarmament.

➡️ Advocated a responsible and human-centric approach on AI and deeper cooperation on cybercrimes and other issues.

➡️ Highlighted that attacks on maritime shipping and civilian infrastructure are unacceptable, and commerce through international waterways should remain safe and unimpeded.

➡️ On South China Sea, underlined that we look forward to a substantive, effective and legally binding CoC fully compliant with UNCLOS 1982 that does not prejudice the legitimate rights of all users.

The warning follows US President Donald Trump's threat to strike Iranian bridges and power plants if Iran attacks commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. Ghalibaf stated that the security of regional energy exports is now at stake, adding that the Strait of Hormuz "will not return to pre-war conditions." The escalating rhetoric comes amid continued US-Iran military tensions, raising fresh concerns over global energy supplies and maritime security in the Gulf.
tweets.content:
BREAKING: Iran Warns of Regional Oil Export Disruption

Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf has warned that if Iran is prevented from exporting oil, no country in the region will be able to export oil either.

The warning follows US President Donald Trump's threat to strike Iranian bridges and power plants if Iran attacks commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

Ghalibaf stated that the security of regional energy exports is now at stake, adding that the Strait of Hormuz "will not return to pre-war conditions."

The escalating rhetoric comes amid continued US-Iran military tensions, raising fresh concerns over global energy supplies and maritime security in the Gulf.

tweets.content:
EAM Dr. S. Jaishankar met with Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi to review the gradual normalization of India-China relations since the October 2024 Kazan summit.

India’s stance remains firm: peace and tranquility in the border areas is the absolute prerequisite for normal ties.

EAM highlighted several concrete steps taken toward normalization over the past few months, including:
▪️ Resumption of direct flights between India and China
▪️ Restarting the Kailash Mansarovar Yatra
▪️ Recommencing bilateral border trade
▪️ Updating the visa regime

Despite the recent border de-escalation, Jaishankar clearly outlined the remaining structural issues that India expects to be addressed:

▪️ Correcting the ongoing trade imbalance
▪️ Securing predictability in supply chains

tweets.title: Government Introduces 50% CAPF Quota for Ex-Agniveers The Government has informed Parliament that 50% of Constable (GD) and Rifleman vacancies in the CRPF, BSF, CISF, ITBP, SSB, and Assam Rifles will be reserved for Ex-Agniveers. Key provisions: • No written examination • Exemption from Physical Standards Test (PST) and Physical Efficiency Test (PET) • 3-year age relaxation for all Ex-Agniveers • 5-year one-time age relaxation for the first Agniveer batch • A dedicated Ex-Agniveer Wing has been created in the Ministry of Home Affairs. The first batch of Agniveers has not yet completed its four-year tenure, so the policy is yet to be implemented through its first recruitment cycle.
tweets.content:
Government Introduces 50% CAPF Quota for Ex-Agniveers

The Government has informed Parliament that 50% of Constable (GD) and Rifleman vacancies in the CRPF, BSF, CISF, ITBP, SSB, and Assam Rifles will be reserved for Ex-Agniveers.

Key provisions:
• No written examination
• Exemption from Physical Standards Test (PST) and Physical Efficiency Test (PET)
• 3-year age relaxation for all Ex-Agniveers
• 5-year one-time age relaxation for the first Agniveer batch

The agreement still requires congressional review before taking effect.

tweets.title: RT by @NetramDefence: A two-day brainstorming workshop on Naval Stealth-Beyond 2030 was organized by NSTL, Visakhapatnam, during 20–21 July 2026, bringing together key stakeholders from DRDO, Indian Navy, Defence Shipyards and Industry to deliberate on future directions in naval stealth technologies. The workshop was inaugurated by Dr. R V Hara Prasad, DG(NS&M) on 20th July in the presence of R Adm Sujit Baxi, DG WDB, and Director, NSTL. During the workshop, NSTL handed over Technology for Fabrication, Installation & Commissioning of the LM2500 Gas Turbine Infrared Suppression System for Naval Vessel to BHEL Over 125 delegates representing DRDO Laboratories, Indian Navy, Defence Shipyards, and Industry attended the workshop. Technical sessions covered a broad spectrum of topics, including Radar Cross Section, Infrared Signature Management, Acoustic Stealth, ELFE and Magnetic Signatures, Integrated Multi-Spectral Stealth Concepts, and Future Naval Stealth Technologies Beyond 2030.
tweets.content:
A two-day brainstorming workshop on Naval Stealth-Beyond 2030 was organized by NSTL, Visakhapatnam, during 20–21 July 2026, bringing together key stakeholders from DRDO, Indian Navy, Defence Shipyards and Industry to deliberate on future directions in naval stealth technologies.

The workshop was inaugurated by Dr. R V Hara Prasad, DG(NS&M) on 20th July in the presence of R Adm Sujit Baxi, DG WDB, and Director, NSTL.

During the workshop, NSTL handed over Technology for Fabrication, Installation & Commissioning of the LM2500 Gas Turbine Infrared Suppression System for Naval Vessel to BHEL

Over 125 delegates representing DRDO Laboratories, Indian Navy, Defence Shipyards, and Industry attended the workshop. Technical sessions covered a broad spectrum of topics, including Radar Cross Section, Infrared Signature Management, Acoustic Stealth, ELFE and Magnetic Signatures, Integrated Multi-Spectral Stealth Concepts, and Future Naval Stealth Technologies Beyond 2030.

tweets.title: We often talk about defence and space together, so here’s an interesting comparison. A few days ago, Vikram-1 made its first flight. If we draw a chronological analogy with defence, it is comparable to an LCA MK1 class milestone. However, in defence, we have already invited a private-sector consortium to lead production of AMCA, a programme that, in terms of ambition and complexity, is far closer to SpaceX’s Starship (even though the design authority remains with DRDO). That’s the difference. In space, private industry is first proving itself on smaller launch vehicles before moving to heavy-lift systems. In defence, we’re asking private industry to participate in manufacturing one of our most ambitious combat aircraft programmes from the outset. This is exactly what we had been saying for years: we haven’t fed our industry the basic tech and forcing them into AMCA. This is why space has revived early success in private efforts.

In a major breakthrough against cross-border arms smuggling, Bikaner Police have arrested three more accused linked to a Pakistan-based drone weapons trafficking network. Investigators revealed that the accused were in direct contact with the main handler and were responsible for retrieving drone-dropped consignments of illegal weapons using precise GPS coordinates before delivering them to designated recipients. The investigation has exposed an organised cross-border syndicate, and authorities are now expanding the probe to identify additional operatives and links across the India-Pakistan border.
